steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage defines two opposing categories — thalassocracy (sea-based) and tellurocracy (land-based) — then introduces the Ottoman Empire, which had both land and sea supremacy. The blank must bridge the clean binary definition to an example that defies it, so the transition must signal a complication or exception.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — For example, tellurocracies have long overpowered their rivals:: "For example" introduces an illustration of what was just said. The prior sentence says thalassocracies and tellurocracies are classified as distinct — the Ottoman Empire does not illustrate that distinction, it contradicts it. ✗
  • Choice B — Likewise, thalassocracies are sea-based powers:: "Likewise" signals similarity or continuation. The sentence that follows is about the Ottoman Empire having both sea and land power, not about sea-based power alone — so "likewise" creates a contradiction in logic. ✗
  • Choice C — In other words, empires are classified according to their source of power:: "In other words" signals a restatement of the previous idea. But the Ottoman Empire does not restate the classification framework — it complicates it by fitting both categories at once. ✗
  • Choice D — Of course, the distinction is not always so neat:: "Of course" acknowledges a known complication, and "not always so neat" directly flags that the clean thalassocracy/tellurocracy binary has exceptions — exactly what the Ottoman Empire illustrates. ✓
That matches Choice D — Of course, the distinction is not always so neat:.
